name: Coalition Scope and Advocacy Depth
lever_classification: governance — who participates in advocacy and how political pressure is mobilized
consequences: A broader coalition spanning civil society, academia, security experts, and elected officials amplifies political pressure on Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID decision-makers, making supplier-concentration concerns harder to dismiss as niche. However, expanding the coalition risks diluting the technical credibility of the message and introducing competing priorities (privacy, consumer rights, data protection) that may overshadow the resilience and sovereignty framing.
options: Focus exclusively on a tight expert core (mobile security researchers, procurement specialists, regulatory experts) and rely on published reports and formal submissions to influence officials directly without broad-based advocacy.

Build a wide coalition including civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, political parties, and consumer groups, accepting that messaging will be less technically coherent but reaching decision-makers through multiple political channels.

Construct a two-tier coalition: a credible expert steering group that owns technical and policy content, paired with a broader stakeholder council (civil society, elected officials, industry participants) that amplifies the message without controlling substance.
review: The core tension is between technical authority and political reach. A narrow expert coalition commands credibility but lacks political leverage if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID operators decide to ignore the findings. Conversely, a broad coalition dilutes messaging and risks reducing the project to one voice among many competing digital-governance concerns. None of the options adequately address how to maintain technical integrity while scaling political influence—particularly if coalition members prioritize competing reforms (consumer privacy, platform regulation) over the specific supplier-concentration argument.

name: Demonstrator Technical Scope and Credibility
lever_classification: execution — the technical scope and completeness of non-production demonstrators
consequences: A lean demonstrator focused on FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS produces defensible, standards-based evidence without scope creep; a more feature-rich demonstrator (supporting multiple biometrics, hardware tokens, backup codes, multi-factor workflows) increases credibility with security practitioners but risks consuming engineering capacity and delaying publication. Either approach must avoid the trap of appearing production-ready, which invites institutional skepticism and raises liability questions.
options: Deliver a minimal, single-authentication-method demonstrator (FIDO2 passwordless sign-on against a simulated Danish identity provider) that can be completed and reviewed quickly, accepting that it will not demonstrate the full fallback-authentication surface.

Build two separate demonstrators with different technology stacks—one mobile (GrapheneOS/FIDO2) and one desktop/browser (hardware token or backup-code fallback)—to show that secure, standards-compliant authentication is possible across multiple OS environments.

Develop an integrated multi-factor demonstrator that covers passwordless primary authentication, out-of-band fallback, hardware-token support, and recovery codes, positioning the technical evidence as closer to production-grade security posture.
review: The tension is between scope defensibility and institutional persuasiveness. A minimal demonstrator finishes quickly but may be dismissed as too artificial to inform real procurement. A richer demonstrator commands more credibility but delays phase two and risks feature creep, consuming resources that should be allocated to policy engagement and coalition-building. The options do not adequately address how to frame a demonstrator's artificiality as a feature (proof of concept, not product) rather than a weakness—particularly if officials demand security assurances that move the demonstrator toward production-grade certification.

name: Regulatory and Standards Entry Point
lever_classification: governance — which institutional and standards bodies are primary intervention targets
consequences: Engaging primarily through Danish procurement (AltID, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) creates direct institutional influence but faces incumbent operator resistance and may slow if political will shifts. Engaging first or in parallel with EU-level bodies (eIDAS2, European Digital Identity Wallet working groups) amplifies pressure through standards harmonization but introduces longer timelines and reduces direct control over Danish outcomes.
options: Prioritize Danish institutional engagement (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID operator, relevant Folketinget committees) with the strategy that successful domestic procurement language will subsequently align with EU frameworks.

Pursue EU-level standards input and working-group participation from phase one, positioning Danish requirements as instances of broader European supplier-concentration and resilience concerns, with the expectation that harmonized EU language will then pressure Danish procurement.

Split efforts between domestic procurement intervention and EU standards participation in parallel, accepting higher coordination overhead but reducing the risk that a Danish-only strategy becomes isolated if AltID operators resist.
review: The core tension is between speed of domestic policy change and leverage through European standardization. A Denmark-first approach may be blocked by incumbent operators or delayed by procurement cycles; an EU-first approach distributes the work across many jurisdictions and timelines, diluting Danish influence. A parallel approach hedges both risks but stretches the project team and budget thinner. Critically, none of the options address the risk that EU eIDAS2 wallet harmonization may codify platform-provider integration (Apple Wallet, Google Wallet) as a compliance pathway, making it harder to justify platform-neutral requirements as anything other than a Danish outlier preference.

name: Procurement Language and Timing
lever_classification: execution — how and when to integrate supplier-neutrality language into AltID and related procurement
consequences: Securing inclusion of platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements in AltID documents or procurement specifications by month 16–18 creates a binding constraint on future vendor proposals; waiting until after AltID's design is finalized (likely by month 14–16) forces the project to propose retrofit solutions that are costlier and face greater incumbent resistance. However, early procurement influence requires political alignment that may not exist if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has already committed to existing operators.
options: Propose binding procurement language (no exclusive dependence on Apple or Google app stores, mandatory fallback authentication path) for inclusion in AltID's formal technical specification and RFP before month 14.
Aim for non-binding guidance language or recommendations in AltID consultation documents and design-phase reports, accepting weaker institutional pressure in exchange for a lower bar to adoption.

Develop a standalone procurement template and best-practice guidance that other EU member states and Danish public agencies can adopt voluntarily, building momentum through example rather than directly mandating AltID changes.
review: The tension is between binding authority and political feasibility. Binding procurement language (option 1) is most effective but faces the strongest incumbent resistance and requires political champions in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. Non-binding guidance (option 2) is easier to adopt but may be ignored if AltID operators prioritize convenience over resilience. A standalone template (option 3) avoids direct confrontation but risks becoming a museum piece if no jurisdiction adopts it. Critically, none of the options address the timing risk that AltID may announce its technical direction before the project's phase-two demonstrators are complete, leaving the project unable to offer credible technical evidence for its proposed requirements.

name: Incumbent Operator Engagement Strategy
lever_classification: governance — how the project relates to existing MitID and AltID operators
consequences: A transparent, collaborative approach (sharing plans with MitID operator and AltID leads early, inviting feedback, framing the project as resilience-enhancing rather than replacement-focused) may build trust but risks giving incumbents a veto over the project's direction or the ability to pre-emptively co-opt its findings. A more adversarial approach (public framing, media engagement, direct pressure through standards bodies) accelerates political momentum but hardens incumbent resistance and may trigger legal or regulatory countermeasures.
options: Maintain operational transparency with the MitID operator and AltID decision-makers from month 1, presenting the project as a resilience audit rather than a replacement effort, and seek formal feedback loops that treat them as collaborators rather than obstacles.

Pursue the project with minimal incumbent engagement until phase-two demonstrators are complete and phase-three procurement language is ready, then present finished work to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID as a formal policy proposal.

Engage selectively with sympathetic officials inside incumbent organizations (technical staff, privacy officers, contingency planners) while avoiding direct engagement with executive leadership, building internal support that can later pressure organizational positions.
review: The tension is between collaborative legitimacy and organizational independence. Early, transparent engagement risks co-option and gives incumbents time to prepare counterarguments or mobilize political allies; late-stage public presentation risks being perceived as an ambush and triggers stronger defensive reactions. Selective internal engagement is Machiavellian but fragile if internal allies lack organizational authority. None of the options adequately account for the realistic scenario that MitID operator and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership may be genuinely committed to the current platform-provider model for reasons (convenience, cost, existing contracts, regulatory interpretation) that no amount of technical evidence will change—and that the project's real lever is not persuading incumbents, but building external political pressure (through coalition, media, EU bodies) that makes the status quo politically untenable.

name: Demonstrator Independence and Governance
lever_classification: governance — who develops and oversees demonstrator implementation
consequences: Housing the demonstrator development within the project team's research institution ensures technical independence and transparent governance, but ties the work to a single organization's credibility and potentially limits access for external security review. Partnering with multiple institutions or involving an external governance board distributes credibility and improves the appearance of neutrality, but introduces coordination overhead and potential conflicts between institutional interests.
options: Develop both demonstrators in-house at the project's Copenhagen research base, with external security review contracted separately, maintaining tight control over the codebase and timeline.

Establish a demonstrator working group spanning 2–3 Danish research institutions plus international security researchers, with shared code repositories and monthly steering reviews, accepting slower decision-making in exchange for distributed credibility.

Commission one or both demonstrators to an independent, security-qualified contractor or consultancy with established expertise in FIDO2 and OpenID Connect, retaining project oversight but outsourcing implementation risk.
review: The tension is between organizational control and distributed credibility. An in-house approach is fast and focused but concentrates the project's technical reputation in a single institution, making the demonstrators vulnerable to institutional criticism or withdrawal of support. A multi-institution working group distributes risk and credibility but introduces governance complexity and slows decision-making. External contracting insulates the project from development risk but creates a dependency on contractor availability and may reduce the project team's technical ownership of the findings. Critically, none of the options address the risk that regardless of governance structure, incumbent operators and their allies can challenge the demonstrator's relevance by arguing that it does not replicate production MitID security complexity, anti-fraud measures, or certification requirements—and that the governance structure does not materially affect the legitimacy of that critique.

name: Success Metrics and Scope Retreat Logic
lever_classification: governance — how success is defined and what constitutes acceptable fallback outcomes
consequences: Strict adherence to the four success criteria (published report, two working demonstrators, formal written response, inclusion in AltID documents) provides clear accountability but leaves the project at risk of mission failure if AltID operators resist or procurement timelines shift. Adopting contingency metrics (EU standards input, policy publication, coalition consolidation) provides fallback success paths but risks being perceived as mission creep or goal-post-moving if political pressure declines.
options: Maintain the four core success criteria as binding and accept that phase three may result in an announced project failure if AltID procurement does not materially shift by month 18, using the failure as a published case study on institutional resistance to digital sovereignty.

Define both primary success metrics (AltID inclusion, formal written responses) and fallback metrics (EU-level standards engagement, published feasibility report influence on broader EU digital identity policy), allowing phase three to pivot toward EU-level impact if domestic traction weakens.

Focus the project exclusively on technical and policy deliverables (feasibility report, demonstrators, EU engagement) and explicitly de-scope formal procurement influence as an outcome outside the project's control, positioning success as evidence production rather than institutional change.
review: The tension is between accountability and institutional resilience. Strict success criteria provide clear evaluation but set the project up for publicly visible failure if institutional actors (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID operator) choose to ignore the findings. Fallback metrics hedge the risk but introduce the appearance of reduced commitment or goal-post-moving, potentially undermining political credibility. A purely evidence-focused approach (option 3) is honest about what the project can control but abdicates responsibility for translating evidence into institutional change—essentially accepting that the project is a think tank exercise rather than an intervention. The fundamental weakness all options share is that they do not adequately address the underlying political-economy reality: whether AltID procurement shifts depends primarily on whether political actors (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership, the Digital Taskforce, relevant ministers, and Folketinget committee chairs) decide supplier concentration is a priority worth institutional disruption. The project's technical and policy work informs that decision but does not determine it.

name: Demonstrator Trust Anchoring and Assurance Model
lever_classification: execution — what technical-credibility narrative and external validation the demonstrators carry
consequences: Choosing how the non-production demonstrators establish and communicate trust—whether through external security audit, published threat model, simulated regulatory certification, or open peer review—determines whether stakeholders treat them as credible technical evidence or as research toys. If the demonstrators lack a clear assurance story, critics can dismiss them as "not real MitID" and avoid engaging with the fallback-authentication concept. Conversely, over-claiming assurance (e.g., "audit-ready") invites scrutiny on production-readiness claims the project explicitly does not make.
options: Commission a published security audit from a Danish or EU-recognized third party (CFCS, TÜV, or equivalent) focused on architecture, threat modeling, and compliance mapping to eIDAS Substantial, publishing the full audit scope and limitations so reviewers understand this is demonstrator assurance, not production certification.

Establish an open technical review board of 4–5 independent security researchers and protocol experts (domestic and EU-based) who review demonstrator design documents, code, and assumptions on a rolling basis and publish a quarterly technical assessment statement alongside the project deliverables.

Publish detailed threat models and architectural design documents under open review with a public comment period, then issue a self-assessment against eIDAS Substantial requirements, framing the demonstrators as "feasibility evidence" rather than certified products and explicitly naming architectural gaps that production systems would close.
review: Demonstrators need credibility to anchor procurement arguments, but any assurance narrative risks becoming either so rigorous it implies production-readiness (inviting scope creep) or so lightweight it fails to convince procurement officers. The core tension is that security professionals want rigor while policy makers want just enough evidence to justify alternative design. None of the options address the timing mismatch: if the audit or review board takes 3–4 months to complete, it compresses phase two and risks phase three timing if institutional decisions accelerate.

name: Coalition Institutional Anchoring and Continuity
lever_classification: governance — how the coalition maintains participation and institutional leverage across 24 months
consequences: The coalition built in phase one (civil society, academics, advocates) typically fragments if not given formal institutional role or incentive structure. By phase three, key members may have resource constraints, competing priorities, or reduced visibility into procurement processes. Weak coalition continuity means the project's narrative and technical evidence arrive at critical procurement moments without coordinated advocacy, losing timing and impact. Conversely, overstructuring the coalition (formal governance, secretariat, bylaws) diverts resources from core technical and policy work and may create liability if the coalition is perceived as lobbying rather than independent research.
options: Formalize a technical steering committee of 3–4 coalition representatives (one academic, one civil society, one security professional, one sympathetic procurement or policy insider) with quarterly meetings, documented decisions, and a formal relationship agreement, tasking them with reviewing phase two demonstrator scope and phase three engagement strategy.

Establish a lean coalition coordination role (0.5 FTE contracted externally) focused solely on maintaining a coalition wiki, scheduling quarterly all-hands calls, and circulating a monthly digest of policy and regulatory developments relevant to Danish digital identity, keeping partners informed without creating governance overhead.

Create a rotating co-authorship model where each coalition partner commits to co-signing one substantive output (feasibility report, policy proposal, or EU engagement memo) and is named as author; this spreads institutional ownership across partners and creates reputational incentive for continued participation without formal committee structure.
review: Coalition members bring advocacy reach and political credibility, but they also have divergent interests—civil society wants transparency, academics want publications, security experts want technical rigor, and policy insiders may face institutional constraints on visibility. The tension is between keeping the coalition loose enough to attract diverse partners and tight enough to coordinate at critical moments. None of the options explicitly address the risk that coalition members will be pressured or discouraged by their own institutions from participating if the project gains political visibility around procurement.

name: AltID Engagement Timing and Entry Strategy
lever_classification: governance — when and how the project formally engages AltID procurement and decision processes
consequences: The project's phase three impact depends critically on when and how it engages with AltID stakeholders and procurement planning. If phase two demonstrators and reports are ready but AltID's procurement timeline has already locked (design frozen, vendor selection under way), the project becomes an ineffective late-stage input. If the project engages too early (phase one), it risks being dismissed as premature or speculative. The timing and framing of entry also determines whether the project is perceived as independent technical input or as political pressure, which affects receptivity and trust from incumbent operators.
options: Conduct a formal mapping during phase one of AltID's documented decision gates, procurement calendar, and standards review cycle, then time phase two demonstrator completion to align with a scheduled AltID steering or technical committee review point, offering the demonstrators as concrete input to a formal consultation window.

Establish a liaison role (0.5 FTE, filled by a project team member with procurement expertise) who maintains monthly contact with AltID's technical lead or procurement coordinator from month 4 onward, sharing preliminary findings, demonstrator progress, and emerging regulatory signals in a continuous feedback mode rather than waiting for a formal submission window.

Defer formal AltID engagement until phase three, but seed technical discussion in phase one through EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and NSIS compliance forums, building external regulatory pressure and precedent that makes AltID's procurement harder to ignore if procurement language around platform-neutrality or fallback access is already expected in EU guidance.
review: Timing determines impact, but the project has limited control over AltID's internal schedule, which may accelerate, stall, or shift scope based on political or budget cycles independent of this project. The core tension is between securing early feedback (which risks irrelevance if AltID is further along than expected) and banking on late-stage leverage (which risks arriving too late). None of the options account for the risk that AltID may formally reject platform-neutrality as out of scope for their procurement or may claim (falsely or not) that Apple and Google agreements prevent alternative paths.

name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Definition and Scope
lever_classification: product — which fallback authentication architecture the demonstrator will implement and advocate
consequences: Defining the fallback path's scope (browser-based, hardware token, SMS-backup, proximity-based, or hybrid) commits phase two's demonstrator work and phase three's procurement language. A browser-based fallback is easier to prototype but may face user experience objections ("type a code into a browser for critical transactions?"); hardware tokens are more secure but face distribution and cost barriers; proximity-based approaches are novel but unproven in Danish regulatory context. Choosing the wrong fallback scope wastes demonstrator resources and produces evidence that doesn't anchor procurement. Conversely, trying to demonstrate multiple fallback pathways simultaneously dilutes phase two effort and confuses the policy message in phase three.
options: Build the phase two demonstrator around a hardware-token-based authentication pathway using FIDO2 over NFC or USB, supported by a web-based enrollment and recovery flow, positioning this as the most secure and platform-independent fallback option, accepting that distribution and user adoption barriers will be addressed in production phase rather than demonstrator.

Develop a dual-demonstrator approach with a lightweight primary fallback (browser-based one-time password delivered via secure channel) paired with an aspirational secondary fallback (hardware token or proximity mechanism), allowing phase three to present the browser-based option as immediately feasible and the hardware token as a longer-term resilience layer.

Create a "minimalist fallback" demonstrator that authenticates via a decentralized identity standard (W3C Verifiable Credentials or equivalent) over a public blockchain or distributed ledger, positioned as the most radical platform-independence, accepting significant skepticism from traditional procurement officers but anchoring discussion of what true platform-neutrality could mean.
review: The fallback path is the project's core technical deliverable, but the choice of which fallback to demonstrate depends partly on assumptions about user friction, regulatory acceptance, and incumbent resistance that are not fully testable within phase two. The tension is between demonstrating what is technically feasible (hardware token) and what is politically acceptable (browser-based). None of the options account for the probability that procurement officers will reject any fallback as adding complexity to what they see as a solved problem, requiring the project to frame fallback not as a replacement but as a resilience-insurance mechanism.

name: Incumbent Operator Communication and Collaboration Posture
lever_classification: governance — the tone, timing, and channel of communication with the MitID operator and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen
consequences: The project's relationship with MitID's operator (typically the digital-infrastructure unit within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or contracted operator) determines whether the project is seen as collaborative research input or adversarial pressure. If the project frames the demonstrators and report as "proof that you can do better," the operator is likely to respond defensively, emphasizing production constraints and security assurance that justify the current Apple/Google model. If the project avoids engaging the operator until phase three, it risks being caught off-guard by operator counter-narratives or by sudden announcements that MitID is already addressing fallback-access concerns. Conversely, excessive deference to the operator's timeline or priorities can defang the project's independence.
options: Schedule a formal engagement meeting with MitID's technical and policy leadership in month 4, presenting the project's framing as resilience and continuity planning (not criticism), sharing the feasibility report in advance, and requesting their input on regulatory constraints and architectural constraints that the demonstrators should account for, positioning the project as research support rather than disruption.

Maintain arm's-length communication through public consultation and formal policy processes, allowing the project to submit feasibility reports and procurement language through Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's open consultations without bilateral meetings with the MitID operator, preserving independence and avoiding the appearance of backroom negotiation.

Engage MitID's operator indirectly through the coalition and EU-level working groups, ensuring that operator concerns and constraints surface through peer organizations and international bodies rather than direct project communication, allowing the operator to engage the project's ideas without direct confrontation.
review: The operator controls MitID's architecture, deployment timeline, and regulatory compliance strategy, making their buy-in potentially valuable but also risky if they use engagement to influence or derail the project. The core tension is between seeking legitimate technical input (which risks capture) and maintaining independence (which may foreclose collaboration). None of the options address the institutional reality that MitID operators are often evaluated on system stability and incumbent-platform compatibility, creating structural incentives to dismiss fallback alternatives as destabilizing or unnecessary.

name: European Alignment and Standards Escalation Pathway
lever_classification: dissemination — whether and how the project escalates findings to EU bodies and standards forums
consequences: The project's phase three procurement impact is amplified if Danish advocacy aligns with broader European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIWC) requirements and NSIS standards discussions. If the project can cite emerging EU guidance favoring platform-neutral access or resilience mechanisms, Danish procurement officers face harder arguments for ignoring the project's proposals. However, escalating to EU bodies requires careful timing (EU processes move slowly) and institutional positioning (the project must be perceived as contributing technical evidence, not as Denmark's formal negotiating position). Over-emphasizing EU alignment may also dilute focus from Danish domestic procurement levers, which are ultimately the intervention point.
options: Assign a project team member to join the EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working group and NSIS standards bodies starting month 6, positioning the project's research findings as technical input to EU-wide standards discussions, and timing phase three outputs to align with EU consultation windows and published guidance, allowing Danish procurement to cite EU precedent.

Prepare a position paper for publication in month 18 co-signed by project coalition partners and sympathetic EU-based researchers, advocating for resilience and platform-neutral access mechanisms in the EDIWC framework, and distribute it to national digital-identity authorities across the EU, building peer pressure on Denmark's Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.

Keep phase three engagement focused solely on Danish procurement and policy processes, with EU alignment treated as secondary benefit if EU bodies happen to align, avoiding the resource and timing burden of parallel EU advocacy and preserving the project's ability to be nimble and responsive to Danish-specific opportunities.
review: European alignment sounds powerful but is often slow and dilutes focus from the primary Danish intervention point. EU processes require 12–18 months to move from input to published guidance, while Danish procurement decisions may accelerate on their own timeline. The core tension is between seeking legitimacy through EU standards (which takes time and institutional navigation) and focusing resources on the domestic procurement lever (which is more direct but more confrontational). None of the options address the risk that EU-level standards discussions on platform-neutrality will fail or be watered down, leaving the project with neither EU precedent nor Danish traction.

name: Success-Criteria Reframing and Phase-Three Contingency Logic
lever_classification: governance — how the project defines success and adapts phase-three scope if institutional traction is weak
consequences: The project's stated success metrics (published report, working demonstrators, formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, inclusion of platform-neutrality language in AltID documents) assume constructive engagement from authorities and procurement timelines that remain open. If AltID's procurement locks early, if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen responds negatively to the project's proposal, or if political conditions shift, these metrics become unachievable through the project's direct actions. The project must therefore establish contingency success criteria that remain attainable even if formal procurement leverage fails: e.g., publication impact, coalition establishment, EU standards influence, or long-term narrative positioning. Defining these contingencies in advance prevents the project from failing operationally while accidentally succeeding strategically.
options: Establish in month 2 a formal decision tree with the funding body and project team specifying which success criteria are binding (e.g., published feasibility report, demonstrators) versus which are "achieved if possible" (e.g., AltID language inclusion), and define a phase-three scope adjustment trigger: if formal AltID engagement shows weak receptivity by month 16, automatically shift phase-three resources toward EU standards input, long-form publication, and coalition consolidation rather than persisting with Danish procurement focus.

Lock the success criteria to the original definitions but allocate a 10% contingency budget in phase three (350k DKK) for rapid pivots such as accelerated academic publishing, expanded EU engagement, or targeted civil-society media campaigns if procurement leverage appears weak, allowing the project to maximize impact through alternative channels without formally redefining success.

Reframe success criteria in month 1 to emphasize narrative influence and coalition strength as primary KPIs alongside the institutional-engagement metrics, defining success as "the project has shifted the discourse around Danish digital identity to include platform-neutrality and fallback access as legitimate policy concerns, whether or not formal procurement changes result within 24 months."
review: Contingency planning is prudent but creates a risk of mission creep: the project can always claim partial success in narrative or coalition terms, potentially masking failure on the core intervention (procurement language in AltID documents). The tension is between honest contingency planning (accepting that institutional change is uncertain) and maintaining focus (not using contingencies as an excuse to diffuse effort). None of the options address the political reality that success will be judged partly by actors outside the project (politicians, media, industry) who may not accept contingency-based redefinition of what constitutes success.}

name: Platform-Neutrality Language Insertion Point
lever_classification: governance — where platform-neutrality requirements enter the decision-making chain
consequences: Choosing where and how to introduce platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into formal procurement documents, AltID design briefs, or EU standards workstreams directly determines whether these constraints become binding or advisory. Early insertion into AltID's functional requirements increases likelihood of adoption but faces higher incumbent resistance; late insertion into procurement terms reduces resistance but may require costly system redesign or trigger contract disputes.
options: Embed platform-neutrality language in the AltID Phase-1 functional requirements document during the next Digitaliseringsstyrelsen stakeholder-consultation window, positioning it as a continuity and resilience mandate rather than a vendor preference.

Propose platform-neutral authentication as a compliance pathway within the Danish eIDAS2 implementation guidance, allowing operators to demonstrate conformance without mandating a specific architectural choice.

Target insertion in the EU Digital Identity Wallet standards working group, where platform-neutrality can be positioned as a harmonization and interoperability requirement that applies uniformly across member states.
review: Inserting requirements early creates political friction with MitID operators and platform vendors, while late insertion may be too weak to prevent architectural lock-in. None of the options address how to handle already-signed AltID contracts or legacy MitID integrations if platform-neutrality becomes retroactively binding—this creates a transition-management gap.

name: Demonstrator Target Environment and Realism Envelope
lever_classification: execution — which environment the demonstrators authenticate against
consequences: The demonstrators' choice of target environment (simulated Danish IdP, simplified eIDAS2 sandbox, or full MitID integration attempt) sets expectations about technical credibility and timeline-to-production. A simulated environment proves feasibility but invites dismissal as unrealistic; an eIDAS2-aligned sandbox increases EU relevance but delays technical closure; attempting MitID integration risks certification failure and credibility collapse if integrity-validation rules change mid-project.
options: Build the primary demonstrator against a fully simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment that respects eIDAS2 Substantial assurance levels and OpenID Connect semantics, with clear documentation of gaps between simulation and production MitID compliance.

Develop the demonstrator within the emerging EU Digital Identity Wallet reference implementation and sandbox, allowing direct technical alignment with emerging eIDAS2 standards and increasing portability to other member states.

Scope the demonstrator as a proof-of-concept integration with an actual non-critical public-sector test environment (tax authority, municipality) operating under a limited-duration sandbox agreement, accepting higher institutional and procurement friction in exchange for production-grade credibility.
review: A fully simulated demonstrator is technically controlled but vulnerable to dismissal as 'not real'; a full production attempt risks catastrophic failure if MitID's certification or platform-integrity rules change. None of the options plan for the scenario in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator refuses test-environment access—forcing an unplanned pivot to simulation or EU-only alignment.

name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Maturity and Scope
lever_classification: product — the fallback path's technical maturity and assurance level
consequences: Defining the fallback path's maturity level (minimal desktop/hardware-token prototype, hardened standards-based implementation, or pre-production certificate) directly affects its credibility with procurement authorities and resilience-planning stakeholders. A minimal prototype lowers delivery risk but weakens the case for procurement inclusion; a hardened implementation strengthens the argument but increases cost and schedule risk if technical obstacles emerge during integration with eIDAS2 assurance frameworks.
options: Deliver a minimal hardened prototype demonstrating a hardware-token or browser-based fallback path using FIDO2, U2F, or equivalent open standards, with documented threat-model coverage and clear scope boundaries, stopping short of production-grade key management or lifecycle workflows.

Develop a near-production fallback authentication implementation suitable for formal security review and compliance assessment under eIDAS2 Substantial assurance, including key-lifecycle management, identity-provider interoperability, and audit-trail completeness.

Partner with an established EU eIDAS2 implementation or identity infrastructure provider to co-develop the fallback path within their architecture, reducing duplication but introducing dependency on the partner's prioritization and timeline.
review: A minimal prototype avoids cost overruns but leaves procurement authorities uncertain whether fallback authentication is genuinely deployable or merely theoretical. A near-production version increases credibility but runs the risk of discovering insurmountable compliance or integration gaps late in phase two, forcing descoping. Partnership with an external provider reduces risk but surrenders autonomy and scope control—and no such partner may agree to prioritize fallback authentication when their core AltID business is moving forward.

name: Coalition Messaging Anchor and Trust Narrative
lever_classification: dissemination — which narrative anchors coalition advocacy
consequences: Choosing the primary framing for the coalition's public advocacy—resilience and continuity risk, digital sovereignty and democratic accountability, or interoperability and standards harmonization—determines which stakeholders align, which incumbent operators feel threatened, and which EU bodies see Denmark as an ally or outlier. A resilience-first narrative attracts continuity-planning officials and Nordic partners but appears to concede that current platforms are adequate; a sovereignty-first narrative energizes digital-rights advocates but triggers accusations of ideological anti-Americanism; a standards-harmonization narrative aligns with EU institutions but dilutes the urgency of Denmark-specific risks.
options: Anchor the coalition's message on critical-infrastructure resilience, supplier-concentration risk, and documented continuity-planning gaps, positioning platform-neutrality as a risk-mitigation and regulatory-compliance requirement analogous to telecom network resilience or supply-chain diversification.

Lead with digital sovereignty, democratic accountability, and the principle that Danes should not depend on foreign proprietary platforms for access to public services, explicitly addressing the political and jurisdictional risks of Apple and Google's control over app integrity, distribution, and user data.

Frame the initiative as advancing EU interoperability standards and portability rights under eIDAS2, emphasizing that platform-neutral fallback access benefits all member states and contributes to the European Union's strategic autonomy and digital independence from non-EU technology ecosystems.
review: Resilience framing is less polarizing but risks sounding technical and incremental, allowing incumbents to claim minor iterations will suffice. Sovereignty framing mobilizes advocates but invites geopolitical backlash and accusations of protectionism. Standards-harmonization framing aligns with EU processes but may be too slow to prevent Danish AltID procurement from locking in the status quo before consensus emerges. None of the options account for the possibility that incumbent operators will adopt resilience language themselves, co-opting the framing to defend their current architecture as already resilient.

name: Institutional Partnership and Anchor Organization Model
lever_classification: governance — which institution provides the project's primary legitimacy and operational base
consequences: Selecting the project's institutional home and primary partner—a university security research group, an independent public-sector R&D facility, a regulatory authority, or a coalition of private-sector security firms—determines credibility, institutional leverage, regulatory access, and freedom to challenge incumbents. A university base provides research credibility and independence but carries lower direct procurement influence; a public-sector facility offers regulatory access but may face political pressure from incumbent operators; a regulatory-adjacent anchor provides the highest formal leverage but risks institutional capture or neutralizing pressure to avoid controversy.
options: Anchor the project at a Danish university security research group or independent research institution with no direct operational stake in digital identity, maximizing research independence and academic credibility while building relationships with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen through formal research partnerships and published findings.

Establish the project within or in formal partnership with the Danish Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or a related public-sector digital infrastructure body, leveraging direct access to procurement and standards decisions while accepting political constraints and the need for careful stakeholder consensus.

Form a multi-stakeholder consortium including academic security researchers, civil-society organisations, contingency-planning officials, and one or more sympathetic private-sector security firms, distributing institutional risk and creating stronger legitimacy across diverse audiences while introducing coordination overhead and potential conflicts of interest.
review: A university-based project has research credibility but limited authority to influence procurement directly; Digitaliseringsstyrelsen-embedded work has policy leverage but faces pressure to avoid antagonizing MitID operators. A consortium distributes risk but introduces governance complexity and potential dilution of message if partners disagree on tactics or priorities. None of the options plan for the scenario in which the chosen anchor organization's political support shifts during phase two, forcing a mid-project institutional realignment or loss of critical access.

name: External Security Review Timing and Scope
lever_classification: execution — when and how deeply external security review occurs
consequences: Scheduling independent security validation of the demonstrators—early in phase two for course-correction, mid-phase for credibility-building, or post-delivery for third-party certification—affects both project defensibility and timeline feasibility. Early review allows design refinement but delays demonstrator release and invites incumbent operators to claim the process is stalling; late review strengthens credibility but creates risk of discovering architectural flaws too late to fix within phase-two funding. Scope choice (threat-modeling only, code-level security audit, or compliance-assessment against eIDAS2 frameworks) determines the review's usefulness to procurement authorities but not necessarily its cost or timeline risk.
options: Commission a threat-model and architectural security review in month 10, after initial demonstrator design but before major implementation effort, using findings to refine the demonstrator approach and publishing a sanitized summary to establish early credibility with regulators.

Defer comprehensive security audit to month 14, after demonstrator alpha-release, allowing the review to assess both architecture and actual implementation while minimizing rework, but accepting schedule risk and later course-correction.

Plan a formal eIDAS2 Substantial-level compliance assessment by an accredited third-party assessor beginning in month 15, producing a published compliance report that signals production-grade security rigor, while accepting highest cost and latest feedback-integration opportunity.
review: Early review course-corrects design but signals uncertainty and creates optics that the team does not trust its own work. Late review provides credibility but guarantees that correcting significant findings will either spill into phase three or require descoping. Formal eIDAS2 compliance assessment carries the highest credibility weight but also the highest cost and schedule risk—and no assurance that an assessor will be willing to review a non-production demonstrator or that their compliance findings will actually influence AltID procurement.

name: Phase-Three Realism and Contingency Escalation Path
lever_classification: governance — how the project escalates or pivots if Danish procurement interest is weak
consequences: Defining what success and failure look like in phase three—and what happens if AltID procurement does not incorporate platform-neutrality language despite the project's work—determines whether the project pivots gracefully or ends in frustration. A contingency plan focused on EU standards and publication maintains momentum even if Danish procurement fails, but risks appearing to abandon the original mission; escalating to regulatory complaint or political advocacy intensifies pressure on Danish authorities but risks institutional backlash and loss of stakeholder access; a pure publish-and-advocate approach preserves relationships but concedes influence over the critical Danish procurement decision.
options: If formal AltID incorporation is weak by month 16, reorient phase three toward publication of findings, EU-level engagement with the Digital Identity Wallet standards working group, and building longer-term coalition pressure through academic networks and civil-society channels, accepting that Danish impact may be deferred to the next procurement cycle.

Establish a phase-three contingency trigger requiring formal advocacy escalation—including testimony to Folketinget's digital policy committee, formal complaint to relevant EU bodies regarding eIDAS2 compliance gaps, or public campaign highlighting supplier-concentration and resilience risks—if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not committed to platform-neutrality or fallback requirements by month 18.

Design phase three with a hybrid approach: continue formal AltID engagement through month 22 while simultaneously building publication and EU-standards contributions as parallel paths, allowing the project to demonstrate progress on multiple fronts regardless of Danish procurement outcomes.
review: Escalation to public advocacy or regulatory complaint risks alienating Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operators, and future institutional partners, potentially foreclosing dialogue on future identity initiatives. A purely EU-focused contingency plan preserves relationships but publicly signals that the project could not move the needle in Denmark, undermining coalition credibility and participant morale. A hybrid approach appears to hedge all scenarios but requires careful political management to avoid appearing ineffectual or losing focus through dilution. None of the options account for the possibility that a national-security or geopolitical event (e.g., sanctions on US technology, or a platform vendor policy change) will suddenly make platform-neutrality urgent and non-negotiable, retroactively validating the project's work but arriving too late to influence the AltID design window.
